segments.s215-g121: یکی رزم سازم is literal word order: quantifier/indefinite (یکی) + noun (رزم) + verb (سازم). سازم is 1st singular present (classical present used for future/irrealis) 'I make / I will make.' یکی here functions as an indefinite/article-like 'a certain' or 'one' modifying رزم. که immediately follows to introduce a relative clause ('that / which') modifying the رزم just mentioned.
segments.s215-g122: خورشید و ماه ندیدست هرگز چنان رزمگاه is the relative clause begun by که: 'that the sun and moon have never seen such a battlefield.' ندیدست is an archaic/perfect negative formation (ن + دیده‌است) meaning 'has not seen.' هرگز is the adverb 'never' that negates the verb; چنان modifies رزمگاه as 'such / the like of,' so the object seen (or rather not seen) is 'چنان رزمگاه.' Note the subject (خورشید و ماه) precedes the verb as is normal.
segments.s215-g9: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' placing the clause in time. بشنید is a past tense verb 'heard' placed before the subject in poetic word order; هومان is the proper-name subject. خروش سپاه is a genitive phrase 'the roar/cry of the army' where خروش (roar) is modified by سپاه (army).
segments.s215-g10: نشست is a past action here meaning 'alighted / sat down' (i.e., dismounted). از بر is a poetic prepositional sequence meaning 'from off' (از = from; بر = on/upon), and تازی اسپی سیاه is a noun phrase 'the black Tazi/Arabian steed' with تازی (Arabian) qualifying اسپی (steed) and سیاه (black) as final adjective.
segments.s215-g11: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'from the host/army' that sets the source of what follows. بسی کشته is a nominal phrase 'many slain' functioning as the object, and دید is the past-tense verb 'he saw' completing the clause (poetic order places elements somewhat flexibly).
segments.s215-g12: This clause repeats the verb دید at the end for a new observation. بسی is the quantifier 'many'. بیهش is an archaic/contracted pronominal/oblique form (literally something like 'to/for him' or 'of them') marking relation to the speaker/leader. از رزم 'from battle' modifies برگشته (past participle 'returned'), and دید is 'he saw' — together: 'he saw many who had returned from battle.'
segments.s215-g13: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal phrase 'poured down' (فرو = downward, ریخت = poured/flowed). از دیده خون literally 'blood from the eye' marks the source of the flow. بر + ـش here (بر برش) indicates motion onto/against him (poetically 'onto his face/cheek'); the final element is a pronominal/locative attachment rather than the noun 'cut' which is another meaning of برش in other contexts.
segments.s215-g14: یکی here = 'someone' or 'one of them'. بانگ زد is an idiom 'gave/let out a cry' (بانگ = a cry/sound; زد = here 'to utter/sound' rather than physical striking). تند modifies the manner 'sharply/fiercely'. بر لشکرش is a prepositional phrase 'to/against his army' with the possessive suffix -ش on لشکر.
segments.s215-g15: چنین گفت is the formulaic 'thus he said' (چنین = thus; گفت = 'said' — here used as speech-verb). کایدر is an archaic/exclamatory or interrogative particle ('کای در' / 'کایدر') used in older Persian to address or call attention; طلایه نبود is a nominal clause 'the vanguard was not (present)' with نبود as past 'was not'.
segments.s215-g16: This is the admonition clause: شما را is the topicalized object 'you' with the object marker را. ز کین 'from hatred/vengeance' indicates the source, ایچ (ایچ) = 'not at all / nothing', مایه = 'cause/reason', and نبود = 'there was not' (past). Together: 'You had no cause at all from hatred' → 'You have no reason for hatred.'
segments.s215-g97: باید که ... کنیم is a necessity construction: باید (must/it is necessary) + که introduces a subordinate clause whose verb is کنیم (1st person plural subjunctive/optative) meaning 'we do' or here 'we should/must do'. یکی نامه is a noun phrase 'a certain letter/a letter' placed before the clause. زی شه کنیم is a classical ordering meaning 'to the king (شه) we make/send' — کنیم is the matrix verb and is in the subjunctive/optative used after باید. Note: شه is 'king' (title) and زی here functions as a classical preposition 'to/for the king' (archaic placement before the noun).
segments.s215-g98: ز کارش همه جمله آگه کنیم is the content of what the letter should do: ز کارش 'of/concern­ing his affairs' (ز = 'from/of'), همه جمله 'entirely/all together' qualifies آگه کنیم 'we make (him) aware' — آگه کردن = 'to inform/notify'. Word order places the genitive phrase ز کارش first, then adverbial intensifiers (همه جمله), then the verb کنیم (1st pl. subjunctive) as the action required.
segments.s215-g99: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه بنزدیک خسرو is the clause's subject + locative phrase: بنزدیک = 'near/close to' and precedes the person it is near (خسرو). رسد is a poetic present (or imperfective) verb 'arrive/come' — here 'when the letter reaches/comes near Khosrow'.
segments.s215-g100: بدلش is an adverbial/phrasing meaning 'in return/thereupon' (here 'in response to it'). اندرون 'within/inside' marks location; آتشی نو = 'a new/renewed fire' (آتشی is adjectival/nominal form 'fiery' used as noun + نو 'new'). رسد here has the sense 'befall/happen' (a fire will arise). The verb comes last as typical poetic order.
segments.s215-g101: بیاری بیاید is a short clause meaning 'will come to help' or 'help will come' — بیاری (classical/compound of به یاری) indicates 'to aid/for help' and بیاید is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'he/it comes' ('may come' or 'will come' in narrative). گو is the quotative/parenthetical 'they say' (literally 'say'), placed before the proper name پیلتن (here a personal/place name).
segments.s215-g102: ز شیران 'from among the lions' is a classical genitive/partitive phrase meaning 'among the brave ones' (شیران is often metaphorical for brave men). یکی نامدار 'one renowned' is an appositive description of پیلتن (a certain famed individual). انجمن ('assembly') qualifies or names the social context; adjectives follow the noun they modify in classical order here ('یکی نامدار انجمن' = 'one renowned of the assembly').
segments.s215-g103: بپیروزی (ب- prefix + پیروزی) = 'with victory/to victory'; از رزم 'from the battle' is the origin phrase. گردیم is the 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive of گردیدن ('to turn/go/come back') and with باز ('again/back') yields 'we will return'. The construction is classical/pastoral but here expresses a future/expected action.
segments.s215-g104: بدیدار is a prepositional/infinitival phrase 'to (the) meeting/visit' (بدیدار = 'to the audience/meeting'); کیخسرو is the subject (the king). آید is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive 'comes' and together with the following noun نیاز ('supplication/plea') it means 'he comes (to the meeting) in supplication' — the noun after the verb describes the manner/purpose of his coming.
segments.s215-g1: A coordinated nominal phrase listing participants: خود (emphatic 'himself') followed by proper names joined with و, ending in سران 'chiefs/heads' which is the head noun. The sequence is a typical Persian coordination where each conjunct is simply linked by و.
segments.s215-g2: نهادند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن 'to place/set' (they placed/took position). The prepositional phrase بر یال گرزگران follows it as its locative complement: بر 'on/upon' + یال 'mane/crest', modified by گرزگران 'of the mace‑bearers' (the noun after functions as possessive/specifier). Order is fairly literal: verb with its locative phrase.
segments.s215-g3: بسوی + NP expresses motion/direction 'toward' (بسوی سپهدار پیران became 'toward the spahbed Piran'). شدند here is used as a past intransitive marker 'they moved/turned' (classical usage: بسوی ... شدند = they moved toward ...). سپهدار is a title 'commander'.
segments.s215-g4: چو = 'when' (temporal). بقلب is the old/prepositional form b- + قلب meaning 'into the heart' or 'in the heart of'. سپه = 'army/host'. بر + زدند is the collocation 'to strike upon' (زدند = past 3pl of زدن 'to strike'), so the clause means 'when a fire struck into the heart of the host.'
segments.s215-g5: چو ... شد is a temporal clause 'when ... became'. دریای خون is a genitive phrase 'sea of blood' (دریا + -ی), and همه رزمگاه means 'the whole battlefield' (همه as adjective 'entire/whole' modifying رزمگاه). The verb شد 'became' links the metaphorical predicate to the battlefield.
segments.s215-g6: خروشی (a noun) 'a roar/clamor' is the subject; برآمد (past) 'arose/issued forth' is the verb. بلند follows as an adverb/adjective 'loud' modifying the manner of the sound, and از سپاه 'from the army' is the source phrase. Word order is stylistically marked but grammatically standard in poetry.
segments.s215-g7: درفش سپهبد is a genitive phrase 'the banner of the spahbed/commander'. بدو 'to him/before him' is an indirect/objective locative. نیم شد is a predicative phrase 'became half/was halved' (نیم as adjective 'half' + شد 'became'), describing the state of the banner—an idiomatic poetic way to say the banner was broken/parted or reduced.
segments.s215-g8: دل رزمجویان = 'the hearts of the warriors' (possessive/genitive). پر از بیم شد is a common copular construction: پر 'full' + از بیم 'of fear' + شد 'became', i.e. 'became full of fear'. The adjective precedes از‑phrase and the verb marks the change of state.
segments.s215-g105: This noun phrase begins with the head noun 'سخن' (words/speech) followed by the restrictive modifier 'هرچ رفت' (literally 'whatever went/was said'); adjectives/adverbs 'آشکار و نهان' (openly and secretly) follow to qualify that speech. Word order is noun + relative/qualifying clause + coordinated modifiers, a typical poetic sequence in Classical Persian.
segments.s215-g106: 'بگویم' is first-person singular present/subjunctive used here with a future/performative sense: 'I will tell' or 'let me tell.' What follows, 'بپیروز شاه جهان', is a vocative/title phrase addressing the king ('بپیروز' = victorious, 'شاه جهان' = king of the world) and so functions as the addressee of the verb rather than its subject.
segments.s215-g107: This phrase lists two nominal qualities, 'بخوبی' (goodness/well-being) and 'خشنودی شهریار' (the shah's approval/satisfaction), linked by 'و'. They function as the favorable conditions or blessings invoked in the sentence.
segments.s215-g108: The clause begins with the optative/ jussive 'بباشد' (may it be) expressing a wish; 'بکام شما روزگار' is the predicate: 'روزگار' (time/days) is 'بکام شما' (to your desire/pleasing to you). Overall this is a benediction: 'May the times be pleasing to you.'
segments.s215-g109: 'چنانچون که گفتند' is a causal/similarity clause meaning 'just as they said' or 'as it was said,' with 'چنانچون' introducing the comparison and 'که' linking to the verb 'گفتند' (they said). 'برساختند' is past tense, third-person plural: 'they built/raised/constructed.'
segments.s215-g110: Here 'نوندی' names what was built (likely a proper/technical term); 'بنزدیک شه' ('benazdīk šah') is a prepositional/locative phrase 'near the king' that precedes the verb 'تاختند' (they charged/attacked). The sequence places the place/target before the action, a common Persian word order in narrative.
segments.s215-g111: 'دو لشکر' is the subject ('two armies/hosts'). 'بخیمه فرود آمدند' is a verbal phrase: 'بخیمه' indicates direction/place ('to/at the tents') and 'فرود آمدند' is past plural 'they came down' or 'they dismounted/alighted.' Together it describes the armies' movement to the camp/tents.
segments.s215-g112: 'ز پیکار' (from the battle/fighting) sets the scene; 'یکباره' is an adverb 'all at once/suddenly'; 'دم' (literally 'breath' or 'a moment/utterance' in poetic usage) is the thing produced, and 'برزدند' is past plural verb 'they struck/uttered/let out.' The clause therefore means 'suddenly from the fight they uttered (a cry).' Word order places the source and adverb before the object and verb, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s215-g73: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' and opens the report. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' and appears before the speakers (verb–subject order is common in epic prose/poetry). شیدوش و گستهم are two proper names joined by و ('and'), and شیر is an appositive epithet 'the Lion' modifying (or identifying) the second name; the names follow the verb as its subjects.
segments.s215-g74: که introduces a subordinate (content) clause here: 'that ...'. شد is the past of شدن 'became/happened'. کار پیکار is a noun phrase in which کار (head 'work/affair') takes the following noun پیکار ('battle') to specify what kind of affair it is. سالار دیر places the head noun سالار ('commander, leader') followed by دیر used adjectivally to mean 'long (in time)'; classical word order here is noun + qualifying adjective.
segments.s215-g75: به + personal name marks the indirect object or addressee ('to/for Bijan'). گرازه here follows the name as an epithet or second name (the text treats it like a name/epithet). همی is the archaic/progressive marker (here giving continuous or iterative sense: 'was saying' / 'again said'). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said', and باز at the end is an adverb 'again' (or 'once more') modifying the speech act.
segments.s215-g76: که introduces the reported content ('that ...'). شد is past 'became' and is the main verb of the subordinate clause. کار سالار is a genitive-like construction where کار ('affair/work') is specified by سالار ('of the commander/leader'). لشکر دراز is a noun + adjective (lشکر 'army/host' + دراز 'long'), describing the army/campaign as extended or prolonged.
segments.s215-g77: This is a paired descriptive phrase: هوا قیرگون uses هوا ('the air/sky') qualified by قیرگون (قیر 'tar' + گون, the adjectival/‑like element, meaning 'tar-colored' or 'pitch-black'). و links the next phrase: زمین آبنوس places زمین ('the earth') followed by آبنوس ('ebony' or 'very dark'), again noun + adjectival qualifier. Such noun + adjective order is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s215-g78: همی آمد is a common classical/periphrastic expression where همی is the progressive/continuous marker and آمد is the past of آمدن 'came' (here 'was coming' / 'came'). از دشت is a prepositional phrase 'from the plain'. آوای کوس is a genitive‑like phrase in which آوای ('the sound/voice of') is completed by کوس ('the war-drum/clarion'), so 'the sound of the drum.'
segments.s215-g79: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went/advanced' (بر + رفتند); here it means troops moved forward. گردان is the subject 'troop(s)/squadron(s)'. بر آوای اوی is a prepositional phrase: بر 'upon/at' + آوای 'the voice/call' + اوی an archaic possessive referring back to the caller ('his/him'), so 'at his call' or 'on his sound.'
segments.s215-g80: ز خون بود is an archaic order meaning 'there was (from) blood' — ز here is the poetic 'from/of', خون 'blood', بود past of 'to be' ('was/there was'). بر دشت is 'upon the plain' (بر + دشت). هر جای جوی literally 'every place [a] stream' — هر جای 'everywhere/each place' and جوی 'stream', meaning streams (of blood) flowed everywhere. Word order is descriptive and somewhat compressed, typical of epic style.
segments.s215-g49: Here 'یکی' is an adverbial classical use meaning ‘at once’ or ‘in one stroke’. 'حمله بردند' is a past tense verb phrase (بردند, 3rd plural past) meaning ‘made/carry out an attack’. Word order places the verb before the subject phrase 'هر سه' (‘all three’); 'به هم' (= 'به' + 'هم') functions together as an adverbial ‘together/against one another’.
segments.s215-g50: 'چو' introduces a temporal/simile clause here = 'when'. 'برخیزد' is a present/subjunctive/3rd‑person form meaning 'rises/gets up' used to describe a simultaneous action. 'از جای' = 'from the place of'; noun phrase 'شیر دژم' = 'a grim/sullen lion' with the adjective after the noun in classical Persian order. The clause as a whole is a simile: 'as when a grim lion rises from its place.'
segments.s215-g51: 'ندیدند' is past negative ('did not see'); the indefinite/negative 'کس' here functions as 'no one'. 'یال اسپ و عنان' is a coordinated object: 'the horse's mane and the reins' (classical Persian places the possessed noun before the possessor without an izāfe in poetry). The conjunction 'و' links the two objects.
segments.s215-g52: 'ز' is the poetic variant of 'از' meaning 'from/because of'. 'تنگی' = 'narrowness' (space/crowding). 'بچشم اندر آمد' is a verbal phrase meaning literally 'came into the eye' or 'struck the eye' (ب- prefix + noun + اندر آمد = entered); the subject of this clause is 'سنان' (the spear), which follows. The line thus explains that, due to the cramped space, the spear entered/struck the eye.
segments.s215-g53: 'چنین گفت هومان' = 'Hōmān said thus' (classical word order can put the verb before the name). 'به آواز تیز' is an adverbial phrase formed with 'به' + noun + adjective meaning 'in a sharp/keen voice' (here 'به' marks the manner: 'with/by a voice that is sharp').
segments.s215-g54: 'که' introduces the content of his speech ('that/that is'): 'نه جای جنگست' = 'this is not a place of battle' (نه = negation placed before the predicate). 'جنگست' is the older compounded verb/adjective 'is war/for fighting'. The phrase 'و راه گریز' is appended as a second predicate: 'nor a way/path for flight/escape.'
segments.s215-g55: 'برانگیخت' is a past tense 3rd‑person verb 'roused/aroused'. The agent/subject 'بخت بد' ('ill fortune' or 'bad fate') follows the verb, a common poetic inversion. 'از جایتان' means 'from your places' with the possessive suffix '-تان' 'your'. Together: 'ill fate has roused you from your places.'
segments.s215-g56: 'که' begins a subordinate/result clause and 'تا' here has a purposive/result sense 'so that' or 'so that it may be that...'. 'بر تن' = 'upon the body' (بر + تن). 'بدکنش' is a compound noun 'the ill‑doer' or 'one of bad conduct'; the following 'بد' qualifies the misfortune. 'رسد' is a present/subjunctive/3rd‑person verb meaning 'may reach / befall' (i.e. 'so that the evil destined for the ill‑doer may befall his body').
segments.s215-g89: سطر reads as a single clause: 'یکی رزم کردند' = 'they fought a battle/one battle.' یکی functions as an indefinite 'a certain/one' (not the numeral emphasis), رزم is the noun 'battle' and کردند is the past tense 3rd-person-plural verb 'they did/made' (here 'they fought'). تا introduces the temporal limit 'until' and attaches to the following noun phrase چاک روز ('the cleaving/tear of day'), a poetic expression for dawn or daybreak (چاک used as noun 'cleft/tear').
segments.s215-g90: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' پیدا شد is a two-word predicate: پیدا (visible/apparent) + شد (became) — 'became visible/appeared.' از introduces the source 'from,' and چرخ گیتی فروز is a poetic noun phrase 'the heaven/sky' (چرخ) + 'world' (گیتی) with the adjective فروز 'bright/illuminating' following; in poetry adjectives commonly follow the noun. The clause gives the time when the fighting stopped (when the bright world appeared from the sky).
segments.s215-g91: سپه is the subject 'the host/army.' بازگشتند is past tense 3rd-person-plural 'they returned/withdrew.' یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/altogether' placed postverbally for emphasis. ز (poetic form of از) marks the origin 'from' and جنگ is the object 'the battle/war.' The clause literally: 'The army returned, entirely, from the battle.'
segments.s215-g92: کشیدند is past 3rd-plural transitive 'they drew/moved' (here used for moving/marching troops). لشکر is the object 'the army/host.' سوی introduces the direction 'toward,' followed by کوه تنگ 'the narrow mountain' (کوه + adjective تنگ after the noun, normal in poetry). The sequence shows action (moved) + object + directional phrase.
segments.s215-g93: بگردان is an imperative 'turn!' or 'turn back!' (imperative form of گردان/برگردان) — a direct command. چنین 'thus/so' indicates the manner of speaking and pairs with گفت 'said/spoke.' Note poetic word order: the imperative (the spoken words) appears, then the report 'چنین گفت' and finally the speaker 'سالار طوس' (subject after the verb, common in poetry).
segments.s215-g94: که begins a subordinate clause (here giving a temporal or circumstantial range). از is 'from'; گردش is 'the circuit/rotation/course' (a genitive construction: 'the course of X'). مهر here is used as a proper name/deity (indexing the sun/Mithra in classical usage), so گردش مهر = 'the sun's course.' تا is 'until' marking the other end of the range, and زخم کوس is a poetic noun phrase 'the stroke/blow of the drum' (زخم used metaphorically as 'blow' and کوس = war-drum). Together: 'from the sun's circuit until the drum's blow.'
segments.s215-g95: سواری 'rider/horseman' is the head noun, followed by the restrictive relative clause introduced by کز (contracted که از) meaning 'that/from whom' or 'whom from' — here marking source 'among you.' چنین is 'such/so' describing the rider. شما is 'you,' and دیده‌ام is the perfect 'I have seen.' The relative clause postmodifies سواری: 'a rider such as I have seen among you.'
segments.s215-g96: ز is the poetic short form of از 'from.' کنداوران (here given as 'excavators' in the senses) is the source group 'from the excavators' (in context likely a group name or epithet). هیچ is used adverbially 'never/no one/nothing' and negates نشنیده‌ام 'I have heard' (present perfect negative sense: 'I have heard none/never heard of'). The implied object (such a rider) is understood from the preceding clause.
segments.s215-g25: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth' standing before its nominal complement for poetic word order. خروشیدن is an infinitive used as a verbal noun 'roaring' and کرنای is the noun 'trumpet' in a genitive relation (the ezāfe is omitted in poetry): together 'the trumpet's roaring'.
segments.s215-g26: بهر سو is a directional phrase 'toward every side' (بهر + سو). برفتند is past tense, 3rd plural 'they went/proceeded', and the logical subject گردان 'troop(s)' follows the verb (postverbal subject is common in poetic prose). ز جای is an ablative phrase 'from (their) place/position'.
segments.s215-g27: گرفتندشان is a past tense verb 'they seized' with the object clitic -شان 'them' attached; یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/at once' modifying the verb. اندر میان is a locative phrase 'in the midst' (اندر = in/within, میان = middle).
segments.s215-g28: سواران ایران is a genitive construction 'the horsemen of Iran' (ایران = the people/land of Iran). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', and شیر ژیان is a poetic compound 'living lions' or 'lions full of life' where ژیان qualifies شیر.
segments.s215-g29: چنان is an adverb 'so/such', modifying the verb phrase that follows. آتش افروخت is a past tense 'kindled (a) fire' (افروخت = kindled/ignited). از ترگ و تیغ is an ablative 'from rags (or tatters) and from swords' specifying the sources of the fire/sparks.
segments.s215-g30: که introduces a result clause 'so that/that'. گفتی همی here is an archaic idiom meaning roughly 'one would say' or 'it seemed one might say' (گفتی is an archaic verbal expression and همی is a modal/emphatic particle). گرز بارد ز میغ is a poetic image 'maces would fall/rain from blades' — بارد is an older-form verbal idea meaning 'would fall/rain' (optative/resultive).
segments.s215-g31: This is a list: شب تار 'dark night' (تار is a poetic adjective 'dark' here), و شمشیر 'and sword', و گرد سپاه 'and the dust of the host' (گرد + genitive سپاه = 'the army's dust'). The nouns are coordinated by و; گرد here means 'dust' rather than the verbal sense.
segments.s215-g32: This line uses the paired negation نه ... نه ... 'neither ... nor ...' with two predicates: ستاره (subject) + پیدا (predicate adjective 'visible') and ماه (subject) + تابنده (predicate adjective 'shining'). The structure means 'neither star visible nor moon shining.'
segments.s215-g65: نه ... آمدیم is a negated past/perfect statement: نه + [place/adverbial] + آمدیم = 'we did not come ...'. ایدر (here) is an adverbial location placed before the preposition phrase. به پیکار و جنگ uses به + two coordinated nouns (پیکار and جنگ) to indicate purpose/role ('for/into battle and war'). آمدیم is 1st-person plural past (we came), placed finally as is normal in Persian clauses.
segments.s215-g66: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause here (giving a reason: 'for/because'). خیره is a predicative/adverbial adjective meaning 'stunned/astonished' describing the state in which they came. بکام is a prepositional/construct-like phrase meaning 'to the will/pleasure (of)', and نهنگ is the possessor/agent in this phrase (literally 'whale' but used as a powerful figure). آمدیم repeats the verb 'we came' for emphasis; it is the same past 1pl form used to conclude the clause.
segments.s215-g67: دریغ is an interjection/exclamatory noun ('pity'/'alas') placed at the clause head. آن is a demonstrative ('that/the') pointing to what follows. در و گاه is a coordinated noun phrase: در = 'gate/portal' and گاه = 'time/court/occasion' (here 'gate and court/time' of the king). شاه جهان is a genitive phrase 'king of the world' modifying گاه (i.e. the royal gate/court). The whole phrase is a lament ('Alas for that ...').
segments.s215-g68: که begins a relative/explanatory clause ('that' / 'that it be that'). گیرند is 3rd person plural present/subjunctive from گرفتن, functioning here to express the (feared) action 'they seize' or 'should seize' — subjunctive/optative nuance is common in classical Persian. ما را uses the enclitic object marker را with the pronoun ما ('us') as the object. کنون ناگهان are adverbs ('now' + 'suddenly') modifying گیرند.
segments.s215-g69: تهمتن is a proper name/epithet (Tahmātan) in subject position. به + [place] here marks location ('in/to Zabulestan'); زاولستانست appears to be a fused form with the copula -ست ('is') attached, meaning 'is in Zabulestan' (textual corruption/variant is noted). و زال appends Zal as a coordinated (co‑present) name: 'Tahmatan is in Zabulestan, and Zal (is there).' Word order places the locative phrase and copula immediately after the name, as is common in poetry.
segments.s215-g70: شود is 3rd person singular present/subjunctive of شدن used as a copular/auxiliary here meaning 'becomes' or 'comes to be'. کار ایران is a genitive nominal phrase 'the affairs of Iran' serving as the grammatical subject/topic of the predicate. کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. تال و مال are two nouns coordinated as the predicate complement: تال ('marsh/pond') and مال ('property/goods') — together giving the state/result into which Iran's affairs have fallen.
segments.s215-g71: همی آمد is a repeated/continuous past tense formula meaning 'kept coming' or 'was continually coming' (همی adds iterative/continuous aspect). آوای + [names] is a genitive/construct: 'the voice/cry of Gopal and the drum' — آوای governs the coordinated nouns گوپال و کوس. The verb آمد follows the subject phrase as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s215-g72: بلشکر is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'with an army' or 'in multitude' (setting the circumstance). همی again gives iterative/continuous/emphatic sense. دیر شد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: دیر ('long/late') + شد ('became') yields 'it was long/delayed' or 'they were delayed for a long time'. گیو و طوس are the coordinated proper names (Giv and Tous) which are the logical subjects/experiencers of the delay.
segments.s215-g57: This is a coordinated nominal phrase: 'سه' is the numeral preceding the head noun 'جنگ‌آور' (three warriors). The conjunction 'و' links that noun to the following noun phrase 'خوار مایه سپاه' (literally 'lowly/ despised mâyé of the host'), where 'مایه سپاه' functions as a compound meaning a portion/contingent of the army and 'خوار' is an attributive adjective meaning 'lowly/despised'.
segments.s215-g58: Verb phrase: 'بماندند' is past-tense 3rd-person-plural (they remained / were left). 'یکسر' is an adverb modifying the verb meaning 'entirely'/'all together'. 'بدین' is the compound of the preposition 'ب(ه)' + demonstrative 'دین' meaning 'to/at this' (here split in the word list), and 'رزمگاه' is the locative noun 'battlefield'. The whole phrase means they all remained at this battlefield.
segments.s215-g59: 'فراوان' is an adverb meaning 'much/abundantly' modifying the verb that follows. 'ز رستم' is a prepositional phrase 'from/of Rostam' marking the source/theme of memory. 'گرفتند' is perfective past 3rd-person-plural 'they took/received' (here idiomatically 'they took/held remembrance of'). 'یاد' is the noun 'remembrance/memory' governed by the verb 'گرفتند'.
segments.s215-g60: This clause is elliptical and emphatic. 'کجا' ('where') introduces a relative/locative idea; each 'داد' is the past-tense 3rd-person singular of 'دادن' ('gave' / 'performed') used here in the sense of 'acted' or 'struck'. 'در جنگ' ('in battle') is the locative phrase, and 'هر جای' ('every place') repeats the idea for emphasis. Overall: 'wherever in battle he acted, he acted everywhere,' i.e. he distinguished himself in every place of battle.
segments.s215-g61: 'ز شیدوش، وز بیژن' are two coordinated 'from' phrases; the second 'وز' is an archaic contraction of 'و + ز' = 'and from'. 'گستهم' is a 1st-person-singular past tense verb 'I wandered / I ranged', a poetic shift of voice (the poet/narrator inserting himself). Grammatically it links the two sources ('from Shidūsh and from Bijan') with the verb 'گستهم'.
segments.s215-g62: 'بسی' is an adverb meaning 'much' or 'a great deal'. 'یاد کردند' is past-tense 3rd-person-plural 'they made/they remembered' (idiomatic 'they recalled'). 'بر' here marks the topic/measure ('with regard to'), and 'بیش و کم' is the idiom 'more and less / to varying degrees'. The phrase says they recalled these matters greatly, to varying extents.
segments.s215-g63: 'که' introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('that' / 'for that reason'). 'باری' here means 'once' or 'on one occasion'. 'کسی را' is the indefinite person marked by the object marker 'را' (the person affected), and 'ز ایران سپاه' is 'an army from Iran' (ز + place + noun). The clause sets up 'on one occasion someone from the Iranian host...'.
segments.s215-g64: 'بدی' here functions as a noun/nominalized action meaning 'harm / wrongdoing' (used idiomatically for 'did harm'). 'یارمان' is a possessive compound 'یار' + 'مان' = 'our companion/ally'. 'اندرین' ('in + this') is a demonstrative locative 'in this', and 'رزمگاه' is 'battlefield'. Together the phrase means that the Iranian host once harmed one of our companions in this battlefield.
segments.s215-g113: طلایه (singular) is the subject 'vanguard'. برون آمد is the verbal phrase 'came forth' (برون = 'out/forth', آمد = 'came'); Persian here places the verb after the noun phrase. از هر دو روی is a prepositional source phrase 'from each/both sides' with هر دو = 'both' and روی used in the sense 'side/flank' (poetic word order places the source after the verb).
segments.s215-g114: بدشت is a directional/location phrase meaning 'to/onto the plain' (poetic order: direction appears before the agentive/source). از دلیران پرخاشجوی is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from the brave, quarrelsome ones' with the adjective پرخاشجوی following the plural noun دلیران; the whole indicates who made the sortie.
segments.s215-g115: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. هومان is the proper name. رسید is the past verb 'reached/arrived'. اندــران (an archaic fused form of اندر + ان) means 'in/at that place' or 'therein' and links to رزمگاه 'battlefield' as the locative complement.
segments.s215-g116: ز here is the poetic partitive/ablative 'of/from' and introduces what was (not) seen: کشته 'the slain' (i.e. dead bodies). ندید is the negative past 'did not see' and ایچ is an emphatic 'nothing/not at all'. بر دشت راه is a locative phrase 'upon the plain's way/ground' (poetic word order puts the location at the end). The whole means 'he saw not any of the slain on the plain.'
segments.s215-g117: به پیران چنین گفت = 'he said thus to the elders' (به + indirect object پیران; چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت here is the verb 'said'). کامروز 'this very day/today' begins the reported clause. گرد in this poetic context denotes the 'turn' or 'lot' (turn of fortune) and heads the clause that follows (an archaic use not reflected by the common senses 'around/dust').
segments.s215-g118: نه ... گشت negates the turning: نه بر آرزو گشت = 'it did not turn toward desire' (بر آرزو = 'toward desire', گشت = 'turned/became'). گاه نبرد ('the time/hour of battle') follows as a nominative phrase indicating the domain to which the turn has actually gone — poetically: 'it did not turn to desire; (it is) the time of battle.'
segments.s215-g119: چو آسوده گردند = 'when they/it became at ease' (چو = 'when', آسوده = 'relieved/at ease', گردند = plural past 'became'). گردان ما is the subject 'our troop/division'; the verb precedes the subject here (poetic inversion). The plural verb form reflects collective or plural agreement.
segments.s215-g120: ستوده modifies the following nouns: 'our praised horsemen and men'. The possessive ما 'our' attaches postpositively to the coordinated noun phrase سواران و مردان, a common classical Persian order: adjective → nouns → possessive.
segments.s215-g81: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing two sources joined by و. گردان here means an array or company (poetic for a gathered sound/host) modifying نیو (the ney, a reed‑flute), so گردان نیو = 'the array/assembly (or swelling sound) of neys'. Then ز نیروی چنگ repeats 'from the power/force of the harp' (نیروی = 'force/energy', چنگ = 'harp'). The two 'ز' phrases together give the sources or causes mentioned in the next clause.
segments.s215-g82: تو is the 2nd‑person singular subject 'you'. گفتی is the past 2nd‑person form of گفتن 'you said/you called out' (classical/past narrative). برآمد is a past/simple perfective 'arose/issued forth'. ز دریا is the prepositional phrase 'from the sea', and نهنگ is the noun 'whale' (literal or figurative). Together this yields direct reported speech: 'you said, “A whale rose from the sea.”'
segments.s215-g83: بدانست is a past verb 'knew/perceived' and appears before the name (inverted order): 'بدانست هومان' = 'Houmān perceived/learned'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that', آمد is the past 'came' and سوار is the noun 'horseman/rider' (subject of آمد). The clause reads: 'Houmān perceived that a rider had come.'
segments.s215-g84: همه is the plural subject 'all/everyone'. گرزور here is best read as 'mace‑bearer' (the person bearing a گرزور), بود is past 'were/was' and the coordination with و شمشیردار yields two predicates: 'were mace‑bearers and (were) sword‑bearers' — i.e. they carried maces and swords.
segments.s215-g85: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دانست is past 'knew' or 'perceived'. کامد is an archaic present/past form meaning 'comes/was coming' or 'arrives' and with ورا ('to him') gives 'that he was coming to him'. یار طوس is 'Tūs's companion/ally' (یار governing طوس). Overall: 'When Tūs's ally perceived that he was coming to him...'.
segments.s215-g86: همی is an adverbial intensifier (here giving immediacy/'straightaway' or emphatic action). برخروشید is a past 3rd‑singular verb 'he burst forth/roared/rose up'. برسان is the verb راسانـدن/رساندن in a form meaning 'to cause/bring about' and with the object کوس ('war‑drum') means 'caused the drum to be sounded' or 'had the drum beaten'. The sequence describes his immediate, noisy reaction and the sounding of the war‑drum.
segments.s215-g87: This clause has two parallel verbless/auxiliary constructions: سبک شد عنان = 'the reins became light' (i.e. loosened/eased) and گران شد رکیب = 'the rider became heavy' (i.e. the rider's weight pressed down or he sat heavily). شد is the past auxiliary 'became' linking the adjectives to their nouns; the و coordinates the two resulting statements.
segments.s215-g88: بلندی here denotes a spring/leap ('a rising' or 'a springing up'). که introduces a clause of reason or result: دانست = 'he knew' and باز از نشیب = 'how to come back/up from the descent' (باز = back/again, از نشیب = 'from the slope/descent'). Together: 'he sprang up, for he knew how to recover from the descent.'
segments.s215-g41: دهید is an imperative/2pl (or hortative) 'give'. ار is the archaic conditional particle (like اگر = 'if') used here in a compressed, poetic instruction. The two nouns بگرز and بژوپین are instruments/objects being supplied (the first is the word for a mace/club formed with a b- prefix; the second is an archaic weapon or shield name); the second دهید repeats the imperative rhythmically and governs the following object clause. Word order is verb — (conditional particle) — objects — repeated verb, a typical compact poetic economy.
segments.s215-g42: سران را: سران (the chiefs/heads) is marked by را as the definite direct object. ز خون is a poetic genitive/partitive 'of/from blood' specifying the material or colour of the crowns. تاج بر سر نهید is a verb phrase with نهید (نهاد stem) in the imperative/jussive 'place/put' taking تاج as its object and بر سر as the locative 'on the head.'
segments.s215-g43: چنین 'thus' modifies the speech. گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said' but the lemma senses given list discourse-noun meanings, so in context it is the past verb 'said'. با + names (گیو و رهام) indicates accompaniment 'with Gīv and Rohām.' طوس (Tūs) appears after the verb for poetic emphasis (verb–object/adjunct–subject order is common in classical lines).
segments.s215-g44: که introduces the content clause ('that'). شد is the past copular/verb 'became' (here 'came to be' / 'fell into'). جان ما is the syntactic subject 'our life/being.' بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly / surely.' بر + فسوس here marks the relation 'into / under (the power of) deceitful whispering' — a poetic use of بر meaning 'to/into' — and فسوس means insidious whispering/idle deceitful talk.
segments.s215-g45: مگر here functions as an exception/optative particle introducing the wish/hope (classical texts often begin such optatives with مگر). کردگار سپهر بلند is a compound noun phrase 'the lofty Maker (Creator) of the sky/heavens,' where بلند is an epithet 'lofty/exalted' modifying سپهر (heavens). The whole phrase is the agent (God) invoked to rescue them.
segments.s215-g46: رهاند is a 3rd‑person verb meaning 'deliver/release' and here has an optative or hortative force ('may he deliver'). تن و جان ما 'our body and life' are the direct objects (joined by و). زین گزند is the source/ablative phrase 'from this harm' — note that زین here is a contracted poetic form (standing for از این) and گزند means 'harm/damage.'
segments.s215-g47: اگر نه opens a counterfactual/alternative 'if not.' بچنگ is a prepositional/compound phrase meaning 'into the grasp/talon' (ب + چنگ), so بچنگ عقاب اندریم means 'we are in the eagle's grip.' اندریم is a form meaning 'we are in / we lie within' (اندر + suffix) — the whole is a present/state clause 'we are in the clutch of the eagle.'
segments.s215-g48: وگر is another conditional 'but if / or if.' زیر دریای آب اندریم literally 'we are under the sea of water' — زیر 'under,' دریای (genitive) 'of the sea,' آب 'water' (poetic pleonasm, 'the waters of the sea'), and اندریم again 'we are in/within' — together describing drowning or being submerged. The clause parallels the previous one as the second dire alternative.
segments.s215-g17: بهر یک از ایشان is a distributive prepositional phrase meaning "for each one of them." ز ما سیصدست is a verbless or existential phrase where ز ما ("of/from us") supplies the group and سیصدست (poetic contraction of است) gives the number: "there are three hundred of us." Word order (prepositional phrase + numeric predicate) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s215-g18: به آوردگه is a locative phrase (به + آوردگه) meaning "at/to the place (of arrival)" used here as "at the sleeping-place." خواب و خفتن pairs a noun and the infinitive to indicate the state/activity "sleep and lying-down," and بدست is used adverbially to indicate being occupied with or having something at hand (here: being at their sleeping-places). The whole segment gives the location/state of those three hundred.
segments.s215-g19: هلا is an interjection (a battle-cry: "Hala!/Come on!"). The noun phrase تیغ و گوپالها is the coordinated object ("swords and the Gopals/cowherds/Gopal-people") and برکشید is past tense, third-person plural, meaning "they lifted/drew" (here, "drew [their weapons]"). In this narrative line the verb is a simple past narration: "they cried and drew their swords."
segments.s215-g20: سپرهای چینی is a noun phrase (plural) "Chinese shields." The sequence بسر در کشید is a verbal expression meaning "(they) drew/put (them) on the head" (بسر = به سر, "on the head"; در کشید = "drew/put on"). کشید is again past third-person plural. The whole means they placed the shields upon their heads as protection.
segments.s215-g21: ز هر سو is an adverbial phrase "from every side." بریشان is the combination بر + ایشان (toward them/on them) and بگیرید is a second-person-plural imperative or a hortatory/subjunctive-like form meaning "take/seize"; راه is the object "the road/the way." The phrase commands: "From every side, take the way toward them" (i.e., surround or approach them from all sides).
segments.s215-g22: کنون is the temporal adverb "now." کز is the relative/causal "that/for" introducing the reason clause. بره is "lamb/innocent," بر کشد is a verbal phrase (بر + کشد) meaning "will draw/strike upon" (here used as a present/future sense in narrative), and تیغ ماه is a possessive compound "the sword of the moon" (i.e., a shining sword or a sword poetically qualified by 'moon'). The clause gives the motive: "now, for the moon's sword will strike the lamb."
segments.s215-g23: رهایی is the noun "release/freedom/escape." نباید is an impersonal modal meaning "there must not / there should not be." که introduces the subordinate clause and یابند is third-person plural present/subjunctive "they find/obtain." هیچ is a negative quantifier "any/anything/none." Together the line expresses a prohibition: "There must be no escape such that they find anything" → "They must not find any escape."
segments.s215-g24: بدین سان (written here split) is an adverbial phrase "in this way/thus." چه is rhetorical/interrogative "what/why." باید here means "is necessary" or "ought (to be)" and introduces the existential question about necessity; درنگ is the noun "delay/tarry" and بسیچ (here) means "many/mustering/large numbers" or generally "much/many" (used here in the sense of mustering/gathering). The whole asks rhetorically: "In this way, what need is there for delay or for much mustering?"
segments.s215-g33: This segment begins with the poetic preposition ز (from), followed by جوشن (jūshan, 'cuirass/armor') and the pronoun تو ('you'). گفتی here functions as the 2nd‑person past tense 'you said' (an ordinary past form used in narrative). ببار is an optative/imperative‑flavored verb 'let fall / pour' (a short, emphatic form used in poetry), and اندرند is a 3rd‑person plural present/stative 'they are/are within' (used to indicate they come/are inside). The overall order is prepositional phrase → speech verb phrase → result clause, typical of Ferdowsi's compressed, poetic syntax.
segments.s215-g34: ز is again the preposition 'from'. تاری here is the noun 'darkness' (not the instrument or tar‑player). بدریای is an adjectival/possessive compound meaning 'moon‑like / of the full moon‑sea' (poetic epithet formed by ب + دریای or an adjective); it modifies whatever follows. قار is a rare/obscure lexeme in this line (likely a proper name or an onomatopoetic epithet in the poem), and اندرند is the same 3rd‑person plural verb 'they are/enter inside' repeating the idea of intrusion. The clause therefore gives a second origin/source ('from the darkness of the moon‑like sea of Qar') and repeats the stative/ingressive verb.
segments.s215-g35: بلشکر here is a prepositional/compound phrase meaning 'with the army' or 'in full force' (literally 'with the host') and functions as a circumstantial adjunct. چنین 'thus' links the manner of speech. گفت is the narrative past 'said' (not rendered in the provided sense list, so marked as -1); هومان is the speaker's proper name. که introduces the reported content/clause that follows; بس is an interjection/exclamation 'enough!' or 'no more' marking a halt or dismissal.
segments.s215-g36: ازین is the fused preposition + demonstrative 'from these' (از + این). مهتران is a plural noun 'the chiefs/greater ones' (a poetic plural form; context shows social superiors). مفگنید is an archaic/formal verb form (given as uncertain in the lexicon data) that here functions as the main predicate of the reported clause; its exact lexical nuance is archaic, but grammatically it is a 2nd/3rd‑person plural/formal present in the narrative pattern. ایچ کس is a frozen negational phrase meaning 'no one' (ایچ = nothing/none, کس = person), so the overall clause asserts that 'from these chiefs no one (…).' The clause is left incomplete here for rhetorical effect.
segments.s215-g37: همه 'all/everyone' is the direct object/topic. پیش من is a prepositional phrase 'before me / in my presence' where پیش governs the noun من. دستگیر is used as a noun/adjective meaning 'the seized / prisoners' (literally 'seized ones') and is the thing to be brought. آورید is a 2nd‑person plural imperative 'bring' (a standard command form in epic address). The order is imperative verb finalizing the command after the object and locative.
segments.s215-g38: نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not / should not' introducing prohibition. که is the subordinating particle 'that' linking to the clause explaining the prohibition. خسته is an adjective 'tired / exhausted' describing those not to be brought. بتیر is the fused prepositional phrase به + تیر 'by/with arrow(s)', indicating condition/cause (i.e., 'wounded by arrows'). آورید is again the 2nd‑person plural imperative/infinitive 'bring' repeated to emphasize the prohibition. Together the clause forbids bringing in people exhausted or pierced by arrows.
segments.s215-g39: چنین 'thus' again frames the manner of speaking. گفت is the narrative past 'said' (not represented in the provided senses list, marked -1). لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. ببانگ is an adverbial compound 'with a cry / in a shout' (ب + نَغْ/نَگ—here 'with a cry'), and بلند is an adverb/adjective 'loud' qualifying the cry. Together they describe that the army spoke out loudly.
segments.s215-g40: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing what was shouted. اکنون 'now' is a temporal adverb. به before بیچارگی marks direction/treatment 'into/into a state of' and بیچارگی is the noun 'misery / helplessness' (the state into which the addressee is placed). دست بند is a compound meaning 'hand‑fetter / bond on the hands' (دست 'hand' + بند 'fetter/band') and functions as a verbal noun phrase implying 'bind their hands' or 'put them in bonds'. Grammatically, the clause is an exclamatory imperative content quoted after the introductory verb.

sentences.s215-s61: I will make a battle the like of which the sun and moon have never seen.
sentences.s215-s5: When Hūmān heard the roar of the army, he dismounted from the black Arabian steed.
sentences.s215-s6: He came; from the army he saw many slain, and he saw many who had returned from battle.
sentences.s215-s7: Blood poured down from his eye onto his cheek; someone gave a sharp cry to his army.
sentences.s215-s8: Thus he said: 'The vanguard was not there; you have no cause for any hatred.'
sentences.s215-s49: We must write a letter to the king so that we inform him fully of all his affairs.
sentences.s215-s50: When the letter comes near Khosrow, in reply a new fire will arise within him.
sentences.s215-s51: They say that Piltan will come to help — a renowned man among the lions (brave warriors) of the assembly.
sentences.s215-s52: We shall return from the battle in victory; at the audience Khosrow will come in supplication.
sentences.s215-s1: He himself, Giv, Goudarz, and several chiefs took their places atop the mace‑bearers' crests.
sentences.s215-s2: They turned toward the commander Piran; when a fire struck into the heart of the host.
sentences.s215-s3: When the battlefield became a sea of blood, a loud cry rose up from the army.
sentences.s215-s4: The spahbed's banner was halved before him; the hearts of the warriors became full of fear.
sentences.s215-s53: I will tell whatever words went forth, both openly and in secret, O victorious king of the world.
sentences.s215-s54: May the days be to your liking, with the king's goodness and approval.
sentences.s215-s55: Just as they had said, they built (raised) a Nond/Nawand, and, coming close to the king, they charged.
sentences.s215-s56: Two hosts came down to the tents; from the fighting, suddenly they let out a cry/uttered (a sound).
sentences.s215-s37: Thus said Shidush and Gostaham the Lion: that the commander's battle was prolonged.
sentences.s215-s38: He again said to Bijan Goraze that the leader's army's affair had become prolonged.
sentences.s215-s39: The air was pitch-black and the earth ebony; from the plain came the sound of the drum.
